case_name: No.4 Is A Mother Of The Deceased Sri. M. ... vs No.1 Has Filed The Written Statement To ... on 19 October, 2016
court: IN THE COURT OF THE IX ADDL. SMALL CAUSES AND ADDL. MACT., BANGALORE, (SCCH-7)

compensation_breakdown.interest: 9% p.a. from date of petition till payment
compensation_breakdown.notes: Breakdown by heads awarded in the judgment (loss of dependency, consortium, care and guidance, funeral, love and affection, transport, loss of estate).

facts: On 01.04.2015 at about 10:15 p.m. near Chandu Motors, Singasandra on Bangalore Main Road, a Mini Bus bearing registration KA-51-C-114 allegedly driven in a rash and negligent manner dashed into the deceased M. Eshwarappa while he was walking on the extreme side of the road. The deceased (aged about 32 years) was taken to LIVE 100 Hospital but succumbed on the way; post-mortem was conducted at Victoria Hospital. Petitioners (wife, minor daughter and parents-in-law) filed a claim under Section 166 MV Act against ICICI Lombard (insurer) and M/s. Megha Travels (owner) seeking Rs. 40,00,000/-.
arguments_claimant: Claimants argued the death was caused by rash and negligent driving of the Mini Bus KA-51-C-114 and relied on FIR, charge sheet, MVI report and eyewitness testimony. They claimed the deceased was a security guard earning approx. Rs.11,970.55 per month and sought future prospects (50%) under Rajesh and Sarla Verma principles and total compensation of Rs.40,00,000/-.
arguments_respondent: Respondent No.1 (insurer) denied maintainability and contested liability, alleging non-compliance with policy conditions: driver without valid/effective driving licence, lack of permit/fitness, failure to produce documents and non-compliance with Section 134 and Section 158(6) requirements; contended claimed amount was excessive and interest should not exceed 6%. The insurer reserved the right to contest under Section 170 and Section 149(2) if owner colluded or was ex parte.
ratio_decidendi: The court addressed whether the insurer could be held liable where policy was admitted but certain policy-violation allegations were raised. The Tribunal held that "at the time of accident, the Respondent No.1 was an Insurer and the Respondent No.2 was a R.C. Owner of the offending Private Mini Bus... and its Insurance Policy was valid, which covers the date of accident." The court further found that "There is no allegation leveled by the Investigating Officer in Ex.P.3 Charge Sheet as against the driver... that, he was not having a valid and effective driving licence" and that "The violation of the terms and conditions of the admitted Insurance Policy by the Respondent No.2 is not proved by the Respondent No.1." Applying these findings, the court held that "the Respondent No.1 being an Insurer and the Respondent No.2 being a R.C. Owner ... are jointly and severally liable to pay the above said compensation and interest to the Petitioners" and concluded "Since, the Respondent No.1 being an Insurer it shall indemnify the Respondent No.2."
final_order: Petition partly allowed; Petitioners entitled to Rs.25,95,346/- with interest at 9% p.a. from date of petition till payment payable by Respondent No.1 (ICICI Lombard). Respondent No.1 to deposit amount within one month; distribution ordered in ratio 50:20:10:20 with specified FDs and advocate fee Rs.1,000/-.
summary: This is an MACT claim under Section 166 arising from a fatal road accident on 01.04.2015 at Singasandra, Bangalore where a mini bus hit a pedestrian, M. Eshwarappa (aged 32). The Tribunal found the bus driver and vehicle responsible and, because the insurer had a valid policy covering the date of accident and allegations of policy breach were not proved, held the insurer jointly and severally liable with the owner. The claim was partly allowed and the court awarded Rs.25,95,346/- with interest at 9% p.a., directing payment by the insurer and providing directions for distribution and FDs.
embedding_summary: MACT-7, Bangalore, M.V.C.No.2107/2015 (19 October 2016). Claim under Section 166 MV Act arising from accident on 01.04.2015 near Singasandra, Bangalore Main Road: Mini Bus KA-51-C-114 (Megha Travels) struck pedestrian M. Eshwarappa (died; aged 32). Petitioners (wife, minor daughter, parents-in-law) v ICICI Lombard (insurer) and owner. Insurer contested liability alleging driver without licence, no permit/fitness, and non-compliance with Section 134/Section 158(6); court found no allegation by IO that driver lacked licence and no proved policy violation. Court applied Sarla Verma and Rajesh (2013 ACJ 1403) principles (50% future prospects for below-40 permanent employment), awarded Rs.25,95,346/- with interest at 9% p.a., held insurer and owner "jointly and severally liable" and ordered insurer to deposit amount and indemnify owner. Cases cited: Rajesh, Sarla Verma, Reshma Kumari, National Insurance v Pushpa; statutes discussed include Sections 166, 134, 158(6), 149(2), and criminal provisions in FIR (Sections 279, 304A IPC).
legal_principles: Where an insurance policy is admitted and covers the date of accident, insurer is liable to pay if alleged policy breaches are not proved
Insurer and registered owner are "jointly and severally liable" to compensate victims when vehicle and driver are found responsible
Absence of allegation by investigating officer that driver lacked a valid driving licence undermines insurer's defence of policy breach on that ground
Application of Sarla Verma/Rajesh principles: 50% addition for future prospects for deceased below 40 in stable employment
Deduction of one-third for personal expenses of the deceased when calculating loss of dependency
Tribunal may direct structured distribution (immediate payment and fixed deposits) for minor and other dependents
sections_cited: Section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1989
Section 134(c) of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988
Section 170 of the Motor Vehicles Act
Section 149(2) of the Motor Vehicles Act
Section 134(a) and (b) and 187 of I.M.V. Act
Section 168(1) Motor Vehicles Act (discussed in Rajesh)
cases_cited: Rajesh and Others V/s. Rajbir Singh and Others, 2013 ACJ 1403 (Supreme Court)
Reshma Kumari and Others V/s. Madan Mohan and Another, (2013) 9 SCC 65 (cited)
National Insurance Company Limited V/s. Pushpa and Others, (2015) 9 SCC 166 (cited)
Smt. Sarla Verma and Others V/s. Delhi Transport Corporation and Another, 2009 (Sarla Verma)
Union of India and Others V/s. S.K. Kapoor, (2011) 4 SCC 589 (cited)
MFA 706/2015 (MV) High Court of Karnataka At Bengaluru (The Karnataka State Road Transport Corporation V/s. Smt. Shanthamma and Another) (cited)
bm25_text: No.4 Is A Mother Of The Deceased Sri. M. ... vs No.1 Has Filed The Written Statement To ... on 19 October, 2016 IN THE COURT OF THE IX ADDL. SMALL CAUSES AND ADDL. MACT., BANGALORE, (SCCH-7) 2016 On 01.04.2015 at about 10:15 p.m. near Chandu Motors, Singasandra on Bangalore Main Road, a Mini Bus bearing registration KA-51-C-114 allegedly driven in a rash and negligent manner dashed into the deceased M. Eshwarappa while he was walking on the extreme side of the road. The deceased (aged about 32 years) was taken to LIVE 100 Hospital but succumbed on the way; post-mortem was conducted at Victoria Hospital. Petitioners (wife, minor daughter and parents-in-law) filed a claim under Section 166 MV Act against ICICI Lombard (insurer) and M/s. Megha Travels (owner) seeking Rs. 40,00,000/-. Claimants argued the death was caused by rash and negligent driving of the Mini Bus KA-51-C-114 and relied on FIR, charge sheet, MVI report and eyewitness testimony. They claimed the deceased was a security guard earning approx. Rs.11,970.55 per month and sought future prospects (50%) under Rajesh and Sarla Verma principles and total compensation of Rs.40,00,000/-. Respondent No.1 (insurer) denied maintainability and contested liability, alleging non-compliance with policy conditions: driver without valid/effective driving licence, lack of permit/fitness, failure to produce documents and non-compliance with Section 134 and Section 158(6) requirements; contended claimed amount was excessive and interest should not exceed 6%. The insurer reserved the right to contest under Section 170 and Section 149(2) if owner colluded or was ex parte. The court addressed whether the insurer could be held liable where policy was admitted but certain policy-violation allegations were raised. The Tribunal held that "at the time of accident, the Respondent No.1 was an Insurer and the Respondent No.2 was a R.C. Owner of the offending Private Mini Bus... and its Insurance Policy was valid, which covers the date of accident." The court further found that "There is no allegation leveled by the Investigating Officer in Ex.P.3 Charge Sheet as against the driver... that, he was not having a valid and effective driving licence" and that "The violation of the terms and conditions of the admitted Insurance Policy by the Respondent No.2 is not proved by the Respondent No.1." Applying these findings, the court held that "the Respondent No.1 being an Insurer and the Respondent No.2 being a R.C. Owner ... are jointly and severally liable to pay the above said compensation and interest to the Petitioners" and concluded "Since, the Respondent No.1 being an Insurer it shall indemnify the Respondent No.2." Petition partly allowed; Petitioners entitled to Rs.25,95,346/- with interest at 9% p.a. from date of petition till payment payable by Respondent No.1 (ICICI Lombard). Respondent No.1 to deposit amount within one month; distribution ordered in ratio 50:20:10:20 with specified FDs and advocate fee Rs.1,000/-. This is an MACT claim under Section 166 arising from a fatal road accident on 01.04.2015 at Singasandra, Bangalore where a mini bus hit a pedestrian, M. Eshwarappa (aged 32). The Tribunal found the bus driver and vehicle responsible and, because the insurer had a valid policy covering the date of accident and allegations of policy breach were not proved, held the insurer jointly and severally liable with the owner. The claim was partly allowed and the court awarded Rs.25,95,346/- with interest at 9% p.a., directing payment by the insurer and providing directions for distribution and FDs. MACT-7, Bangalore, M.V.C.No.2107/2015 (19 October 2016). Petitioners (wife, minor daughter, parents-in-law) v ICICI Lombard (insurer) and owner. Insurer contested liability alleging driver without licence, no permit/fitness, and non-compliance with Section 134/Section 158(6); court found no allegation by IO that driver lacked licence and no proved policy violation. Court applied Sarla Verma and Rajesh (2013 ACJ 1403) principles (50% future prospects for below-40 permanent employment), awarded Rs.25,95,346/- with interest at 9% p.a., held insurer and owner "jointly and severally liable" and ordered insurer to deposit amount and indemnify owner. Cases cited: Rajesh, Sarla Verma, Reshma Kumari, National Insurance v Pushpa; statutes discussed include Sections 166, 134, 158(6), 149(2), and criminal provisions in FIR (Sections 279, 304A IPC). M.
